title: Tahini chocolate cake
subtitle: Rich chocolate cake with a tahini swirl through the batter
excerpt: A deeply chocolate loaf cake with tahini stirred through the batter in thick ribbons before baking. The tahini and chocolate bake together into a dense, fudgy crumb with a sesame-bitter note that cuts the sweetness. No frosting; the top caramelises on its own.
sourceNotes: Chocolate and tahini is a combination associated with Middle Eastern and Eastern Mediterranean baking and confectionery traditions. The sesame and cocoa pairing is well established in Israeli and Palestinian home baking. This recipe is synthesised from the modern domestic tradition of tahini-swirl baked goods, drawing on the established technique of folding tahini (not fully mixing it) into a chocolate batter so the sesame ribbons remain distinct during baking.

recipe.freezeNotes: Cool completely before freezing. Slice and freeze flat with baking paper between slices.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The cake improves on the day after baking: the crumb settles and the tahini flavour deepens. Keeps well-wrapped at room temperature for 3 days.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan oven; a loaf tin bakes evenly at a moderate temperature in 50 to 55 minutes

glossaryTerms.definition: To get distinct tahini ribbons in the baked cake, add the tahini after the batter is mixed and drag a skewer or knife through it once or twice. Do not stir it in fully. The oil in tahini resists blending with a water-based batter, which helps the swirls stay distinct during baking.

content.text: Tahini has enough fat, enough bitterness, and enough character to hold its own against good dark chocolate. The
keeps the tahini from disappearing into the batter. The finished cake tastes of chocolate first, sesame second, and very little else.

Heat the oven to 175 degrees C fan. Grease and line a 2 lb loaf tin. Whisk 200 g light brown sugar with 3 eggs until combined. Stir in 150 g cooled melted chocolate and 120 g melted butter. Add 100 g yoghurt. Sift in 200 g plain flour, 30 g cocoa powder, 1 tsp baking powder and 3 g salt. Fold until just combined.
Add the tahini swirl and bake

Pour the batter into the prepared tin. Spoon 80 g tahini in thick blobs over the top. Use a skewer or thin knife to drag the tahini through the batter two or three times to create streaks. Do not mix it in fully. Bake for 50 to 55 minutes until a skewer inserted in the centre comes out with only a few moist crumbs attached; the top should be set and cracked in places. Cool in the tin for 20 minutes before turning out.

items.solution: The tahini was too well-incorporated. Add it after the batter is fully mixed and fold in no more than twice. A stiff, thick tahini (rather than a runny one) holds its shape better in the batter.
The cake overbaked. Check at 50 minutes: the skewer should come out with a few moist crumbs, not completely clean. The cake continues to set as it cools in the tin.
